text: High-load strength training (unilateral heel raises with a towel under the toes) performed every second day produces superior short-term outcomes compared to plantar fascia-specific stretching for plantar fasciitis at 3 months.
specific_value: 29-point lower FFI at 3 months (95% CI: 6-52, P=0.016)

text: The superiority of high-load strength training over stretching for plantar fasciitis does not persist beyond 3 months, with no significant differences at 6 or 12 months.

Both high-load strength training and plantar-specific stretching combined with shoe inserts lead to substantial improvement in plantar fasciitis by 12 months, with low residual foot function index scores.
specific_value: FFI 22 points (strength) vs 16 points (stretch) at 12 months

text: The high-load strength training protocol for plantar fasciitis consists of unilateral heel raises with a towel inserted under the toes to dorsiflex the metatarsophalangeal joints, performed every second day with progressive loading.
High-load strength training for plantar fasciitis may accelerate early pain reduction and functional improvement compared to stretching alone, making it preferable when faster recovery is desired.
